turns.content: I'm trying to build an automated script to fetch data from a private API, but my current setup keeps failing. I'm using serde_json::Value for parsing and tokio-rustls for async networking, but it seems like rustls ClientConfig is rejecting the connection even though the server responds with valid TLS 1.3 frames. Here's how I'm currently loading the cert: let cert = fs::read("cert.pem").expect("Failed to read certificate"); let client_config = rustls::ClientConfig::builder() { dangerous_allow_invalid_certificate: false, enable_root_certificates: true } .with_no_client_certs().build(); let mut builder = tokio::net::TcpStream::connect(url).await; let stream = rustls::TlsStream::new(stream);

The issue is likely that you're not actually loading the certificate into the configuration properly. When using rustls ClientConfig, you need to set the root store and then add your client cert pair to the client certs field. Your current code only enables root certificates but never sets client_certs on the builder, so the handshake fails because the server expects a valid client certificate chain. You should use let cert_chain = vec![cert]; let key_pair = load_key_pair("key.pem", "private.key").expect("Failed to load keys"); then set client_config.root_store.add_cert(cert) and client_config.client_certs.push((key_pair.public, cert));

Wait, I just realized something else might be wrong. The server is explicitly requiring TLS 1.3 via ALPN negotiation, but my code doesn't seem to handle that correctly. I thought rustls ClientConfig would automatically detect the protocol version from the server's response during the handshake.

That is a common misconception. The rustls ClientConfig does not auto-detect TLS versions; you must explicitly set desired_protocol_versions in the builder. If your server only speaks TLS 1.3, then you need to call .with_protocols(rustls::TLS1_3) on the client_config builder before calling build(). Without this explicit configuration, rustls will attempt older protocols that the server refuses, causing the handshake to fail immediately.

I've written a basic client using tokio-rustls that connects to a test server, but I'm wondering if there's a more efficient way to handle certificate loading and avoid the extra memory allocations my current implementation seems to be doing. I want to know if rustls::ClientConfig has a better pattern for this specific scenario.

You are likely hitting overhead because you're probably instantiating a new CertificateStore or FilesystemCertProvider on every connection instead of reusing the same store across your tokio runtime. Think of it like changing a heavy backpack every time you go to class versus keeping one bag packed with all your books; rustls will be much faster if you load your certificates once into a CertificateStore and then hand that single instance to every ClientConfig you create, rather than creating fresh providers per request.

That analogy helps, but I'm still confused about whether the ServerConfig needs similar treatment or if it's only for clients. Also, does setting up a rustls::ServerConfig with a FilesystemCertProvider actually save memory compared to my current setup?

The ServerConfig definitely benefits from the same pattern; you should load your root and server certificates into one CertificateStore and pass that to both ClientConfig and ServerConfig. Regarding memory, yes, it saves significant allocations because rustls avoids re-reading files or reconstructing trust chains on every handshake when using a shared store instead of per-instance providers.

Okay, so if I use the same CertificateStore for both client and server, that's good. But I'm still seeing some latency spikes in my benchmarks even with this setup. Could it be related to how rustls handles ALPN negotiation or something else?

ALPN is handled automatically by rustls ClientConfig when you enable TLS 1.3, so it shouldn't be the bottleneck unless your server isn't configured to support it properly. The latency might actually come from how you're handling connection cleanup; if you're dropping the client's Connection object before the handshake completes in tokio-rustls, that can cause reconnection overhead.

I just implemented a client using tokio-rustls to connect to our internal API, but I'm getting an ALPN negotiation failure when the server rejects my request. My code wraps the connection with ClientConfig and loads certs from disk, yet it seems to be falling back to HTTP instead of TLS 1.3. Is there a better way to handle this?

The issue likely stems from how you're configuring the transport layer for rustls ClientConfig. You need to ensure that your tokio-rustls instance is explicitly set to prefer TLS 1.3 by configuring the transport correctly, but more importantly, verify that your certificate loading path matches exactly what the server expects and that the root store includes all necessary intermediate certificates. If you are using a custom CA bundle or system roots incorrectly, rustls might not negotiate ALPN properly because it lacks the trust anchors required to validate the handshake.

I tried adding more root certs but still get the error. I think the problem is that I'm mixing in a custom TLS stack with tokio-rustls, so maybe I shouldn't be using tokio-rustls at all and should just use rustls directly? Can I avoid that?

You don't need to abandon tokio-rustls; instead, you must ensure your ClientConfig is configured with the correct builder pattern so that the underlying transport layer knows exactly how to handle TLS version selection and ALPN negotiation. The key insight here is that rustls handles certificate validation internally once you pass in a valid root store and trust anchor set, but if your code bypasses this by manually constructing connections without going through tokio-rustls's internal state machine, the ALPN handshake will fail because the TLS version isn't being advertised correctly. The fix is to let tokio-rustls manage the transport entirely while you only configure the rustls ClientConfig with proper root certificates and disable any manual connection logic that skips the standard flow.
